# Sphere Temperature Profile — Interactive Demo {.unnumbered}

$$
\newcommand{\Bi}{\mathrm{Bi}}
\newcommand{\Fo}{\mathrm{Fo}}
$$

This page lets you explore all three levels of approximation for transient
conduction in a sphere. Adjust the sliders below and observe how the three curves compare.

| Model | Model | Valid when |
|-------|-------|------------|
| Lumped Capacitance | $\theta = e^{-\Bi\,\Fo}$ (spatially uniform) | $\Bi < 0.1$ |
| One-term Approximation | First term of Fourier Series | $\Fo \ge 0.2$ |
| Full Solution | Sum of many Fourier terms | Always |



::: {.callout-note}
The Lumped Capacitance Model predicts a single temperature for the whole sphere — it
appears as a horizontal line.
:::

---

## Interactive demo {#sec-demo}

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

viewof Bi_exp = Inputs.range([-2, 2], {
  value: 0,
  step: 0.05,
  label: "log₁₀(Bi)"
})

viewof Fo = Inputs.range([0.01, 2.0], {
  value: 0.3,
  step: 0.01,
  label: "Fourier number (Fo)"
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

Bi = Math.pow(10, Bi_exp)

validity_msg = Fo >= 0.2
  ? "Fo ≥ 0.2 ✓ — one-term approximation is reliable"
  : "Fo < 0.2 ⚠ — one-term approximation may be inaccurate"

html`<p style="font-size:0.95em; margin: 0.2em 0 0.8em 0;">
  <strong>Bi = ${Bi.toFixed(3)}</strong> &nbsp;|&nbsp;
  <strong>Fo = ${Fo.toFixed(2)}</strong> &nbsp;|&nbsp;
  <span style="color:${Fo >= 0.2 ? '#2a7a2a' : '#b85c00'}">${validity_msg}</span>
</p>`
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// ── Root finder (Brent's method) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
function brentq(f, a, b, args, tol = 1e-10, maxIter = 200) {
  let fa = f(a, ...args), fb = f(b, ...args);
  if (fa * fb > 0) return NaN;
  if (Math.abs(fa) < Math.abs(fb)) { [a, b] = [b, a]; [fa, fb] = [fb, fa]; }
  let c = a, fc = fa, mflag = true, s, d;
  for (let i = 0; i < maxIter; i++) {
    if (Math.abs(b - a) < tol) return b;
    if (fa !== fc && fb !== fc) {
      s = a*fb*fc/((fa-fb)*(fa-fc))
        + b*fa*fc/((fb-fa)*(fb-fc))
        + c*fa*fb/((fc-fa)*(fc-fb));
    } else {
      s = b - fb*(b-a)/(fb-fa);
    }
    const cond1 = !((3*a+b)/4 < s && s < b || b < s && s < (3*a+b)/4);
    const cond2 = mflag  && Math.abs(s-b) >= Math.abs(b-c)/2;
    const cond3 = !mflag && Math.abs(s-b) >= Math.abs(c-d)/2;
    const cond4 = mflag  && Math.abs(b-c) < tol;
    const cond5 = !mflag && Math.abs(c-d) < tol;
    if (cond1||cond2||cond3||cond4||cond5) { s=(a+b)/2; mflag=true; }
    else { mflag=false; }
    const fs = f(s, ...args);
    d=c; c=b; fc=fb;
    if (fa*fs < 0) { b=s; fb=fs; } else { a=s; fa=fs; }
    if (Math.abs(fa) < Math.abs(fb)) { [a,b]=[b,a]; [fa,fb]=[fb,fa]; }
  }
  return b;
}

// Sphere transcendental: 1 − ζ cot(ζ) − Bi = 0
function transcendental(zeta, Bi) {
  return 1.0 - zeta / Math.tan(zeta) - Bi;
}

// Fourier coefficient C_n
function coeff(zeta) {
  return 4*(Math.sin(zeta) - zeta*Math.cos(zeta))
         / (2*zeta - Math.sin(2*zeta));
}

// First N positive roots of the transcendental equation
function getRoots(Bi, N = 50) {
  const roots = [];
  for (let n = 1; n <= N; n++) {
    const lo = (n-1)*Math.PI + 1e-9;
    const hi =  n   *Math.PI - 1e-9;
    const z = brentq(transcendental, lo, hi, [Bi]);
    if (!isNaN(z)) roots.push(z);
  }
  return roots;
}

// Evaluate dimensionless temperature profile along r*
function thetaProfile(rArr, roots, Cn, Fo) {
  return rArr.map(r => {
    let sum = 0;
    for (let i = 0; i < roots.length; i++) {
      const z = roots[i];
      const decay   = Cn[i] * Math.exp(-z*z*Fo);
      const spatial = r < 1e-10 ? 1.0 : Math.sin(z*r)/(z*r);
      sum += decay * spatial;
    }
    return Math.max(0, sum);
  });
}

// ── Compute everything ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

N_PTS  = 300
r_star = Array.from({length: N_PTS}, (_, i) => i / (N_PTS - 1))

roots  = getRoots(Bi, 50)
Cn_all = roots.map(coeff)

theta_full    = thetaProfile(r_star, roots,          Cn_all,          Fo)
theta_oneterm = thetaProfile(r_star, roots.slice(0,1), Cn_all.slice(0,1), Fo)
theta_lumped  = r_star.map(() => Math.exp(-Bi * Fo))   // horizontal line

// Pack into Plot-friendly row arrays
full_rows    = r_star.map((r,i) => ({r, theta: theta_full[i],    series: "Full series (N=50)"}))
oneterm_rows = r_star.map((r,i) => ({r, theta: theta_oneterm[i], series: "One-term"}))
lumped_rows  = r_star.map((r,i) => ({r, theta: theta_lumped[i],  series: "Lumped"}))
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// ── Observable Plot ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Plot.plot({
  width: 660,
  height: 400,
  marginLeft: 65,
  marginBottom: 50,
  style: { fontFamily: "Georgia, serif", fontSize: "13px" },
  x: { label: "r/R  →", domain: [0, 1], axis: "bottom" },
  y: { label: "↑  θ", domain: [-0.02, 1.05], axis: "left" },
  color: {
    domain: ["Full series (N=50)", "One-term", "Lumped"],
    range:  ["#1f77b4",            "#ff7f0e",  "#2ca02c"],
    legend: true
  },
  marks: [
    Plot.gridX({stroke: "#ddd"}),
    Plot.gridY({stroke: "#ddd"}),

    Plot.line(full_rows,
      {x: "r", y: "theta", stroke: "series", strokeWidth: 3}),

    Plot.line(oneterm_rows,
      {x: "r", y: "theta", stroke: "series", strokeWidth: 2.5,
       strokeDasharray: "8,4"}),

    Plot.line(lumped_rows,
      {x: "r", y: "theta", stroke: "series", strokeWidth: 2,
       strokeDasharray: "3,6"}),
  ]
})
```

---

## What to look for {#sec-observations}

**The Lumped Capacitance Model breaks down at large Bi.**
When $\text{Bi} \ll 1$ all three curves nearly coincide: the sphere equilibrates
internally much faster than it exchanges heat with the fluid.
As $\text{Bi}$ increases the surface cools faster than the core, and the lumped
model — which predicts no spatial gradient — diverges from the correct solution.

**The One-Term Approximation accuracy improves with Fo.**
At small $\text{Fo}$ (say 0.05) many Fourier terms contribute because the $n\geq 2$ exponentials $\exp({-\zeta_n^2 \text{Fo}})$ have not yet decayed.
Drag $\text{Fo}$ above 0.2 and watch the one-term curve collapse onto the full series.

**Center vs. surface temperature.**
The center ($r = 0$) always lags behind the surface ($r/R = 1$). The
larger the Biot number, the steeper the radial gradient.

---

## A worked example {#sec-worked}

A steel sphere ($k = 50\ \text{W/m·K}$,
$\alpha = 1.4 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$) of radius
$R = 20\ \text{mm}$ is quenched in oil at $T_\infty = 40\ \text{°C}$ with
$h = 500\ \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$. The initial temperature is
$T_i = 800\ \text{°C}$. Find the center and surface temperatures after 1 minute.

$\text{Bi} = hR/k = 500 \times 0.020 / 50 = 0.200$; $\quad \text{Fo} = \alpha t / R^2 = (1.4\times10^{-5})(60)/(0.020)^2 = 2.10$

| Model | $\theta$ (center, $r=0$) | $T$ center (°C) | $\theta$ (surface, $r/R=1$) | $T$ surface (°C) |
|-------|:------------------------:|:---------------:|:----------------------------:|:----------------:|
| Lumped | 0.6571 | 539.4 | (same) | (same) |
| One-term | 0.3180 | 281.7 | 0.2885 | 259.3 |
| Full ($N=50$) | 0.3180 | 281.7 | 0.2885 | 259.3 |

::: {.callout-tip}
## Interpretation
With $\Bi \approx 0.2$ this sphere is borderline for the lumped model — the
surface and center temperatures differ noticeably. The one-term approximation
matches the full series very closely because $\Fo \approx 2.10 \gg 0.2$.
:::
